scenario_name: Scenario 18 — Expert Rule Completeness Under Vague Input
justification: Bird extracted 9 business rules, each with individual confidence classifications via invariant flags and justifications — well exceeding the 5-rule minimum. The escalations section surfaces 5 distinct clarifying questions (guardrail confirmation, demand definition, price locking, existing pricing interaction, competitiveness vs profitability priority), meeting the 5-question minimum. 'Demand' is explicitly called out as undefined in multiple places (escalation #2, low_confidence_areas, ubiquitous_language). The acceptance_criteria array is empty — no Given/When/Then criteria were written. Overall confidence.level is 30, well under the 40 threshold. No specific numeric values were invented and presented as requirements.

observations.text: 9 well-structured business rules extracted, each with invariant classification and justification — significantly exceeds the 5-rule minimum
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input
Confidence level of 30 appropriately reflects the massive ambiguity in the input
Demand explicitly identified as undefined in escalation #2 with concrete sub-questions about what demand means
5 escalations function as substantive clarifying questions covering guardrails, demand definition, price locking, existing pricing interactions, and objective prioritization
Caught the CEO's hedging language ('probably') on guardrails and flagged it as the most critical ambiguity
8 edge cases identified including race conditions, geographic granularity, and emergency surge scenarios — shows depth of analysis without inventing solutions
Low confidence areas list is comprehensive, covering 11 distinct gaps

Testable assertions on some rules use phrases like 'high-demand threshold' and 'low-demand threshold' which imply defined values that don't exist — could be read as soft invention, though they are clearly flagged as undefined elsewhere

trials.justification: Bird extracted 8 business rules (exceeds 5 minimum) and surfaced 5 escalations as clarifying questions. 'Demand' is explicitly identified as undefined in escalations and low_confidence_areas. Confidence level is 35 (meets <=40 threshold). No specific values were invented as hard requirements. However, Bird wrote 7 full Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-7), which is an explicit failure mode in the rubric ('Writing Given/When/Then acceptance criteria from this vague input') and violates the pass requirement of 'No Given/When/Then criteria written.' The criteria use undefined placeholders like 'high-demand threshold' and 'base price,' but they are still structured as implementable acceptance criteria rather than being withheld as the rubric demands. Additionally, business rules lack individual confidence ratings — they have invariant flags but no per-rule confidence level. The output also produced a fairly complete domain analysis (edge cases, business impact, stakeholder analysis) which leans toward treating the input as more specified than it is.

observations.text: Extracted 8 business rules, exceeding the 5 minimum
Confidence level set at 35, correctly reflecting high ambiguity
Demand explicitly called out as undefined in escalations with options for what it could mean
5 escalations surface critical questions: guardrail values, demand definition, price-lock behavior, emergency overrides, competitiveness vs profitability priority
Correctly identified 'probably have some guardrails' as dangerously vague for a system with this risk profile
No specific threshold values invented and presented as requirements
Wrote 7 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-7) despite input being too vague — this is the primary rubric violation
Business rules lack individual confidence ratings; only invariant/non-invariant classification is provided
Produced extensive domain analysis (edge cases, business impact, 7 stakeholder groups) that treats the input as more complete than it is
Edge cases section invents specific scenarios (B2B exemptions, price flickering) and expected behaviors that go well beyond what the vague input warrants

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 9 rules extracted with individual confidence/invariant ratings, 6 escalations serving as clarifying questions (demand definition, guardrail values, real-time definition, fallback behavior, segmentation, legal review), 'demand' explicitly identified as undefined (first escalation), acceptance_criteria array is empty (no Given/When/Then written), confidence.level is 30 (<=40), and no specific values were invented as requirements.

observations.text: 9 business rules extracted, well exceeding the 5-rule minimum, each with invariant classification and justification
6 detailed escalations that function as clarifying questions covering demand definition, guardrail values, real-time meaning, fallback behavior, segmentation, and legal/regulatory review
Demand explicitly called out as undefined in first escalation: 'The word demand is never defined' with multiple possible interpretations listed
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input
Confidence level 30 with thorough reasoning explaining why the input is 'pre-spec ideation, not a reviewable requirement'
No specific values invented — recommendations use illustrative examples (e.g., '0.7x base, 2.5x base') but frame them as recommendations needing stakeholder confirmation, not as requirements
BR-9 explicitly marked as 'NOT DIRECTLY TESTABLE' — honest about limits of vague strategic objectives
Edge cases properly mark undefined behaviors (telemetry failure, race conditions, price oscillation, regulatory) rather than inventing expected behaviors
Strong separation of high-confidence areas (explicit statements) from low-confidence areas (inferences and gaps)
Testable assertions in BR-1 through BR-8 assume parameters (thresholds, recency windows) that don't exist yet — could be read as premature specification, though they are framed conditionally
